> **(a)** Describe the principle and procedure for the **fractional precipitation of proteins from egg-white / serum using ammonium sulphate**, including the role of temperature, pH, and stepwise salt addition. Mention the expected protein class precipitated at each saturation cut. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the mass of solid ammonium sulphate (MW = 132.14) required to take **200 mL of a 30%-saturated protein solution to 70% saturation** at 20 °C. Use (i) the linear approximation, and (ii) the exact formula that accounts for volume change. Discuss why the two answers differ. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle, Procedure & Expected Fractions (7 Marks):**

**Principle:** Proteins differ in their solubility at high ionic strength, governed by the Cohn equation $\log S = \log S_0 - K_s I$. By incrementally raising the (NH₄)₂SO₄ concentration, proteins with the highest $K_s$ (or lowest intrinsic solubility) precipitate first, allowing *fractional* separation.

**Procedure:**
1. **Clarify** the crude extract by centrifugation at **10 000 × g, 4 °C, 20 min** to remove cell debris and unbroken cells.
2. **Pre-chill** the clarified supernatant to **0–4 °C** on an ice bath, with continuous gentle magnetic stirring. (Low temperature minimises denaturation, slows protease activity, and improves the salting-out efficiency of (NH₄)₂SO₄.)
3. **Adjust pH** to the protein's isoelectric point where possible, since solubility is minimum at the pI.
4. **Slowly add** finely powdered (NH₄)₂SO₄ in small pinches over 30–60 min, allowing complete dissolution and equilibration between additions. *Never add all salt at once* — it causes local supersaturation, co-precipitation of undesired proteins, and possible denaturation.
5. After each saturation increment, **equilibrate for 30 min at 4 °C**.
6. **Centrifuge** at 10 000–15 000 × g, 4 °C, 20 min to collect the precipitated pellet (the *protein fraction* for that cut).
7. Decant the supernatant, **measure its volume**, and use it as the starting material for the next saturation increment.
8. **Re-dissolve** each pellet in a small volume of working buffer.
9. **Desalt** by dialysis or a PD-10 Sephadex G-25 column before storage or further analysis.

**Expected Cuts (egg-white / serum):**
| **Saturation Cut** | **Expected Precipitated Protein Class** |
|---|---|
| 0–30% | Ovalbumin (egg-white); Albumin (serum) |
| 30–55% | Conalbumin (egg-white); α- and β-Globulins (serum) |
| 55–75% | Globulins (egg-white); γ-Globulins / IgG (serum) |
| 75–100% | Residual high-MW proteins; lysozyme only at very high saturation with pH adjustment |

**(b) Mass-Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given: $V = 200$ mL $= 0.200$ L; $S_1 = 0.30$; $S_2 = 0.70$.

**(i) Linear approximation** (per 1 L basis, $m = 533 \cdot \Delta S$):

$$m_{\text{per L}} = 533 \times (0.70 - 0.30) = 533 \times 0.40 = 213.2 \text{ g/L}$$

Scaling to 0.200 L:

$$m_{\text{add}} = 213.2 \times 0.200 = 42.64 \text{ g}$$

**(ii) Exact formula** (volume-change correction):

$$m_{\text{per L}} = \frac{533 \, (S_2 - S_1)}{1 - 0.3 \, S_2} = \frac{533 \times 0.40}{1 - 0.3 \times 0.70} = \frac{213.2}{1 - 0.21} = \frac{213.2}{0.79}$$

$$m_{\text{per L}} = 269.87 \text{ g/L}$$

Scaling to 0.200 L:

$$m_{\text{add}} = 269.87 \times 0.200 = 53.97 \text{ g}$$

**Discussion:** The exact formula predicts ~**53.97 g** while the linear approximation gives ~**42.64 g** — a **~27% under-estimation**. The reason: when solid (NH₄)₂SO₄ dissolves, it contributes a non-zero volume to the solution (≈ 0.3 mL per gram). At high target saturations (here 70%), the volume increase is significant, so the *concentration* of the salt in the new, larger volume is *less* than what the linear formula assumes. Hence more salt is needed to reach the same final saturation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Internal Choice [KTU ESE – Dec 2023 Style]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Cohn salting-out equation** in detail. Sketch (and describe) a graph of **log S versus ionic strength I** for a typical protein. Show, on the same axes, how the curves differ for a "hydrophilic" (e.g., lysozyme) versus a "hydrophobic" (e.g., globulin) protein. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of ammonium-sulphate precipitation as a downstream-processing step, and state **five specific precautions** that must be observed during the laboratory experiment to obtain reproducible results. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Cohn Equation and Graph (7 Marks):**

The Cohn equation is:

$$\log S = \log S_{0} - K_{s} \cdot I$$

A **plot of $\log S$ (y-axis) vs. $I$ (x-axis)** is a **straight line with negative slope** of magnitude $K_s$ and y-intercept $\log S_0$. As $I$ increases (i.e., as more (NH₄)₂SO₄ is added), $\log S$ decreases linearly, meaning protein solubility drops exponentially with salt concentration.

A **hydrophobic protein** (e.g., globulin) has a *large* $K_s$ (steeper negative slope) — it is easily salted-out. A **hydrophilic protein** (e.g., lysozyme) has a *small* $K_s$ (gentler slope) and remains soluble at much higher $I$. Therefore, the lysozyme line lies above the globulin line and crosses the "solubility threshold" at a higher ionic strength.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Ionic Strength I] -->|log S equals log S0 minus Ks times I| Y[Linear Decline]
    subgraph Sketch of Two Curves
    direction LR
    L1[Lysozyme: small Ks gentle slope, line stays high, solubility threshold crossed late] --> L2[Globulin: large Ks steep slope, line drops quickly, threshold crossed early]
    end
```

**Salting-out threshold** is reached when $S$ drops below the practical detection limit of soluble protein (~1 mg/mL) — at this point, visible precipitation occurs. This threshold $I$ defines the saturation cut that captures the target protein.

**(b) Advantages, Limitations, and Precautions (7 Marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Low cost** and easily available reagent.
2. **Gentle** — preserves native protein structure and enzymatic activity.
3. **Concentrates** dilute protein solutions while simultaneously *purifying*.
4. **Removes** low-MW contaminants, nucleic acids, lipids, and proteases.
5. **Easy to scale up** from lab bench to industrial bioreactor.
6. **Long-term storage** — precipitated proteins can be stored as (NH₄)₂SO₄ paste at 4 °C for months without significant loss of activity.

**Limitations:**
1. **Poor resolution** — fractional cuts always co-precipitate neighbouring proteins; final purity typically 10–50%, not chromatographic grade.
2. **Large volumes** of salt must be added, and the product must later be desalted.
3. **Some proteins are denatured** at very high (NH₄)₂SO₄ concentrations or by local osmotic shock.
4. **Corrosive to stainless steel** at high concentration → process equipment must be plastic-lined or specially alloyed.
5. **Not suitable** for proteins that require very low ionic strength for solubility.

**Five Specific Lab Precautions:**
1. **Add salt slowly, in small pinches, with constant gentle stirring at 0–4 °C** to avoid local supersaturation and protein denaturation.
2. **Maintain cold temperature (0–4 °C) throughout** to suppress protease activity, microbial growth, and minimise denaturation.
3. **Pre-calculate the exact mass of salt required** for each saturation cut using the appropriate formula; do *not* estimate by eye.
4. **Equilibrate for at least 30 min** after each salt addition before centrifugation, to ensure complete precipitation equilibrium.
5. **Always measure the post-addition volume** of the supernatant and account for it in subsequent salt-mass calculations; the cumulative volume change is non-negligible.
6. **Use finely powdered analytical-grade (NH₄)₂SO₄** free of heavy-metal contaminants.
7. **Verify % saturation by refractive index** (an Abbe refractometer) for very high-saturation cuts, since the linear approximation breaks down.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't** confuse "salting-out" (precipitation at *high* salt) with "salting-in" (solubility *increase* at *low* salt). The Cohn equation only describes salting-out. The initial "salting-in" region (often linear with opposite slope at very low $I$) is governed by *Debye-Hückel* theory.
> - **Don't** forget to **mention temperature** in the procedure; mentioning only (NH₄)₂SO₄ without specifying 0–4 °C is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> - **Don't** use the linear formula for saturations above 70% — examiner will deduct for not using the exact formula.
> - **Don't** confuse the "mass of salt per litre of *initial* solution" with "per litre of *final* solution" — the standard KTU lab formula uses *initial* volume.
> - Always specify that the protein precipitate is recovered by **centrifugation** (not filtration), and that the supernatant is the *next* starting material.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**
> - **Salting-out** = high-salt-induced protein precipitation; **Salting-in** = low-salt-induced solubility enhancement.
> - **Cohn Equation:** $\log S = \log S_0 - K_s I$ — linear log-solubility vs. ionic strength.
> - **Molecular weight of (NH₄)₂SO₄ = 132.14 g/mol**; **Solubility at 20 °C = 767 g/L**.
> - **Standard KTU mass formula (linear):** $m = 533 \times \Delta S$ g per litre.
> - **Exact mass formula (with volume correction):** $m = \dfrac{533 \cdot \Delta S}{1 - 0.3 \, S_2}$ g per litre.
> - **Hofmeister series:** SO₄²⁻ and NH₄⁺ are both kosmotropic → strong salting-out.
> - **Standard saturation cuts:** 0–20%, 20–40%, 40–60%, 60–80%, 80–100%.
> - **Temperature:** All operations at **0–4 °C** (on ice, with magnetic stirring).
> - **Add salt slowly** in pinches, with **continuous stirring** and **30-min equilibration** between additions.
> - **Centrifuge** at 10 000–15 000 × g, 20 min, 4 °C to recover pellets.
> - **Desalt** the final pellet by **dialysis** (24–48 h, 4–6 buffer changes) or by **Sephadex G-25** gel filtration.
> - **Advantages:** cheap, gentle, scalable, simultaneous concentration + purification, long shelf-life.
> - **Limitations:** low resolution (co-precipitation), denaturation risk at high ionic strength, corrosive to metals.
> - **Precautions:** slow addition, cold temperature, exact pre-calculation, fine powder, equilibration, volume tracking, refractometer check.
> - **Engineering utility:** front-end volume-reduction and enrichment step before chromatography in industrial downstream processing of therapeutic proteins, enzymes, antibodies.
> - **Pitfall:** Linear formula is **only valid for $S_2 < 70\%$**; above this, use the exact formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paper Chromatography – Separation of Amino Acids by Paper Chromatography & Determination of Rf Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL307 - BIOTECHNOLOGY ENGINEERING (BT BRANCHES)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Paper Chromatography – Separation of Amino Acids by Paper Chromatography & Determination of Rf Value

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paper Chromatography (PC)** is a planar partition chromatographic technique in which a strip or sheet of **highly purified cellulose filter paper** acts as the **stationary phase** (specifically, the water trapped within the hydrated cellulose fibers), while a suitable **organic solvent mixture** (mobile phase) ascends or descends through the paper by **capillary action**, separating the components of a mixture based on differential **partition coefficients** between the two phases.

In the context of **Module Experiment 2** of the KTU 2024 Biotechnology Engineering syllabus, the technique is applied to **separate a mixture of amino acids** (the analytes) and to **determine their Retention factor (Rf value)** — a dimensionless constant that quantitatively characterizes the migration behavior of each amino acid under specified experimental conditions.

**Key Standard Metrics (Bolded):**
- **Stationary Phase:** Whatman No. 1 chromatography paper (cellulose matrix holding ~6–8 % bound water).
- **Mobile Phase (Solvent System):** Typically **n-Butanol : Acetic Acid : Water (BAW) in the ratio 4:1:1 v/v** (upper organic layer).
- **Rf Value Range:** Purely **dimensionless**, lies between **0 and 1**.
- **Detection Reagent:** **0.2 % Ninhydrin solution in acetone or ethanol** (yields Ruhemann's purple upon reaction with α-amino groups).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a marathon race held on a sponge track soaked with water.**
> - The **water trapped inside the sponge** = stationary phase (loves polar amino acids like glycine).
> - The **wind blowing across the runners** = mobile phase solvent (carries less polar amino acids faster).
> - Each **runner (amino acid)** has a unique comfort level with water — some love it (and stay back), others hate it (and run ahead with the wind).
> - The **finish line distance traveled** by each runner, divided by the total distance traveled by the wind front, gives the **Rf value** — a numerical fingerprint of that amino acid's personality.

This simple picture instantly conveys *why* separation occurs: each amino acid has a different affinity for water vs. the organic solvent, so they migrate at different speeds.

---

## 1.3 Principle in a Single Line

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Separation occurs because each amino acid distributes itself between the stationary (aqueous) phase and the mobile (organic) phase according to its characteristic partition coefficient (K). The Rf value is a direct, reproducible expression of this distribution."**

---

## 1.4 Classification Context

Paper Chromatography belongs to the broader family of **Partition Chromatography**, which KTU 2024 categorizes alongside:

| Technique | Stationary Phase Form | Mobile Phase Movement |
|---|---|---|
| **Ascending Paper Chromatography** | Water in cellulose (paper) | Solvent moves **upward** |
| **Descending Paper Chromatography** | Water in cellulose (paper) | Solvent moves **downward** |
| Radial / Circular Paper Chromatography | Water in cellulose (paper) | Solvent moves **radially outward** |
| Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) | Silica / Alumina on glass | Solvent rises by capillarity |

The KTU Module 2 experiment uses the **Ascending** mode as it is simplest and most reproducible in a student laboratory.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Partition Theory

The migration of an amino acid spot on the chromatogram is governed by the **Nernst Distribution Law (Partition Law)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Nernst's Partition Law:** A solute distributes itself between two immiscible solvents in a constant ratio of concentrations (at constant temperature), independent of the absolute amount of solute added.

For an amino acid (A) partitioning between the stationary aqueous phase (S) and the mobile organic phase (M):

$$K = \frac{C_S}{C_M}$$

Where:
- $K$ = **Partition coefficient** of the amino acid.
- $C_S$ = concentration of amino acid in the stationary aqueous phase.
- $C_M$ = concentration of amino acid in the mobile organic phase.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Rf Expression (Logical Steps)

**Step 1 — The retardation factor relationship:**
The migration distance of a solute is directly proportional to the fraction of time it spends in the mobile phase. Empirically:

$$R_f = \frac{1}{1 + \alpha \cdot \frac{A_S}{A_M}}$$

Where $\alpha$ is a constant related to the partition coefficient, and $A_S$, $A_M$ are the effective cross-sectional areas of stationary and mobile phases in the paper.

**Step 2 — Simplification to the practical Rf formula:**
For routine analysis at constant temperature and a fixed solvent system, all the constants collapse into a single, measurable ratio:

$$\boxed{R_f = \frac{\text{Distance travelled by the amino acid spot from the origin}}{\text{Distance travelled by the solvent front from the origin}}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical bounds:**
- $R_f = 0$ → amino acid **does not move** (extremely polar, fully retained by water).
- $R_f = 1$ → amino acid **moves with the solvent front** (extremely non-polar, fully soluble in mobile phase).
- $0 < R_f < 1$ → the universal range for all amino acids.

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Rf Values (Critical for KTU Viva)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners frequently test the following factors. Memorize these as they directly influence your experimental Rf.**

1. **Nature of the solvent system** — polarity, pH, and composition.
2. **Temperature** of the chromatographic chamber.
3. **Quality and grade of the chromatography paper** (Whatman No. 1 vs No. 3).
4. **Distance of the origin line** from the solvent reservoir (should be ≥ 2 cm).
5. **Saturation of the chamber** with solvent vapor (atmospheric equilibration for 20–30 min).
6. **pH of the solvent system**.
7. **Concentration of the amino acid sample** (overloading causes tailing).
8. **Direction of solvent flow** (ascending vs descending).
9. **Distance the solvent front travels** (typically 15–18 cm from origin).

## 2.4 Choice of Solvent System — Rationale

The **n-Butanol : Acetic Acid : Water (BAW) 4:1:1** system is preferred because:

- **Butanol** provides moderate non-polar character.
- **Acetic acid** suppresses the ionization of –COOH and –NH₃⁺ groups of amino acids, making them behave as neutral zwitterion-like species with predictable Rf.
- **Water** maintains the stationary phase hydration of the cellulose.

**Standard Rf Reference Values (BAW 4:1:1, Whatman No. 1):**

| Amino Acid | Approximate Rf Value |
|---|---|
| Glycine | 0.26 |
| Alanine | 0.38 |
| Valine | 0.60 |
| Leucine | 0.73 |
| Phenylalanine | 0.68 |
| Tryptophan | 0.66 |
| Aspartic acid | 0.24 |
| Glutamic acid | 0.32 |
| Lysine | 0.14 |
| Arginine | 0.20 |
| Serine | 0.27 |
| Proline | 0.43 |

*(Values may vary ± 0.05 with laboratory conditions.)*

## 2.5 Detection with Ninhydrin — Chemistry Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Ninhydrin (2,2-dihydroxyindane-1,3-dione)** reacts with primary and secondary α-amino groups to produce **Ruhemann's Purple** (diketohydrindylidene–diketohydrindamine, max absorbance ~570 nm). Proline and hydroxyproline (imino acids) yield a **yellow-brown** color instead.

The general reaction:

$$\text{Amino Acid} + 2 \, \text{Ninhydrin} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{NH}_3 + \text{Ruhemann's Purple} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Constant | Expression | Units / Range | Engineering / Lab Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Retention Factor (Rf)** | $R_f = \dfrac{d_{\text{solute}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le R_f \le 1$ | Quantitative identification of amino acids |
| 2 | **Partition Coefficient (K)** | $K = \dfrac{C_S}{C_M}$ | Dimensionless | Fundamental driver of separation |
| 3 | **Nernst Distribution Law** | $\dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = K \;(\text{constant at fixed }T)$ | — | Predicts distribution at equilibrium |
| 4 | **Resolution (Rs)** — adjacent spots | $R_s = \dfrac{d_2 - d_1}{0.5(w_1 + w_2)}$ | Dimensionless | Quality of separation |
| 5 | **Solvent Front Distance (d_solvent)** | Measured from origin to solvent front | cm | Denominator of Rf |
| 6 | **Standard Ninhydrin Concentration** | $0.2\% \; w/v$ in acetone | g/100 mL | Visible detection limit ~ 1 µg |
| 7 | **Chamber Saturation Time** | $\ge 20$ minutes | minutes | Prevents solvent front distortion |
| 8 | **Standard Paper Grade** | Whatman No. 1 | — | Uniform capillary flow rate |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer scripts, **never** quote an Rf value without specifying the solvent system and temperature. A bare Rf = 0.38 is meaningless; always write "Rf = 0.38 in BAW (4:1:1) at 25 °C."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Procedure, Analytical Matrix & Worked Numerical

## 3.1 Reagents, Apparatus & Materials Required

### Reagents
- Standard amino acid solutions (Glycine, Alanine, Leucine — typically 1 mg/mL in distilled water).
- **Solvent system:** n-Butanol : Glacial Acetic Acid : Distilled Water (4 : 1 : 1 v/v) — freshly prepared, upper organic layer used.
- **Ninhydrin reagent:** 0.2 % w/v in acetone.
- Distilled water (chromatographic grade).

### Apparatus
- **Whatman No. 1 chromatography paper** (sheet ~ 20 × 20 cm).
- Chromatographic chamber (glass tank with airtight lid).
- Capillary tubes / micropipettes (5 µL).
- Pencil (HB), ruler, oven / hot-air dryer (~ 100 °C).
- Spray bottle or dipping trough for ninhydrin.
- Glass rod, tongs, fume hood.
- Top-loading analytical balance (if standard weighing needed).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each numbered step below is a **KTU-recordable observation step**. Examiners may ask you to write 8–10 of these in order during the viva or record submission.

### **STEP 1 — Preparation of Chromatographic Paper**
- Cut Whatman No. 1 paper into a rectangle of approximately **15 cm × 20 cm**.
- Handle **only by the edges** (use clean gloves or forceps) to avoid contamination from skin amino acids / sweat.
- Draw a **light pencil line (origin line)** parallel to and **2 cm above the bottom edge** of the paper.
- On this line, mark **3–4 equidistant points** (1.5 cm apart) with pencil dots — these are the spotting positions.

### **STEP 2 — Spotting the Amino Acid Samples**
- Using a fine capillary tube, draw up the **standard amino acid solution** (e.g., glycine).
- Gently touch the capillary tip to the first pencil-marked spot — allow a **tiny drop (~2–3 µL)** to be absorbed.
- **Dry the spot** using a hair dryer / hot air at low temperature (do NOT overheat).
- Repeat the spotting process **3 times** at the same location to concentrate the sample (intermittent drying between applications).
- Repeat the above for the other amino acids (e.g., alanine, leucine) and/or the **unknown mixture** on the remaining spots.

### **STEP 3 — Chamber Saturation**
- Pour the freshly prepared BAW solvent (4:1:1) into the chromatographic chamber to a depth of about **1 cm**.
- Line the inner walls of the chamber with **Whatman filter paper wicks** soaked in the same solvent — this saturates the chamber atmosphere with solvent vapor.
- Close the airtight lid and allow the chamber to **equilibrate for at least 20–30 minutes** at room temperature (~ 25 °C).

### **STEP 4 — Developing the Chromatogram**
- Carefully place the spotted chromatography paper into the chamber such that its **bottom edge dips into the solvent** but the **origin line remains ~1 cm above the solvent surface**.
- Seal the lid tightly to prevent solvent evaporation.
- Allow the solvent to **ascend by capillary action** until the solvent front reaches approximately **3 cm below the top edge** of the paper (~ 90–120 minutes).
- **Do NOT disturb** the chamber during development.

### **STEP 5 — Drying and Visualization**
- Remove the paper from the chamber using tongs.
- Immediately **mark the solvent front** with a pencil before it evaporates.
- Dry the paper in a **fume hood** or hot-air oven (~ 60 °C) to evaporate the solvent completely.
- Spray the dried paper **uniformly** with **0.2 % ninhydrin reagent** (or dip in ninhydrin trough).
- Place the paper in an **oven at 100–110 °C for 5–10 minutes** to develop the colored spots (Ruhemann's Purple).

### **STEP 6 — Measurement and Rf Calculation**
- After color development, **circle each visible spot** with a pencil.
- For each spot, measure:
  - $d_{\text{solute}}$ = distance from origin to the **center of the spot** (in cm).
  - $d_{\text{solvent}}$ = distance from origin to the **final solvent front** (in cm).
- Calculate Rf using the boxed formula in Section 2.

### **STEP 7 — Identification**
- Compare the experimentally determined Rf of the unknown spot with the Rf values of the **standard amino acids** chromatographed on the same paper under identical conditions.
- A match (within ± 0.05) confirms the identity of the unknown amino acid.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiners love numerical Rf problems. This is the exact pattern they set.**

**Question:** In a paper chromatography experiment using BAW (4:1:1) solvent, the solvent front traveled **15.0 cm** from the origin. Three amino acid spots were observed at distances **3.9 cm, 5.7 cm, and 9.0 cm** from the origin. Calculate the Rf value of each amino acid and identify them using the reference table in Section 2.4.

### **Solution — Step-by-Step**

**Step (i): Write the Rf formula.**

$$R_f = \frac{d_{\text{solute}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}}$$

**Step (ii): Substitute the measured solvent front distance.**

$$d_{\text{solvent}} = 15.0 \; \text{cm}$$

**Step (iii): Calculate Rf of Spot 1 (d = 3.9 cm).**

$$R_{f,1} = \frac{3.9}{15.0}$$

$$R_{f,1} = 0.26$$

**Step (iv): Calculate Rf of Spot 2 (d = 5.7 cm).**

$$R_{f,2} = \frac{5.7}{15.0}$$

$$R_{f,2} = 0.38$$

**Step (v): Calculate Rf of Spot 3 (d = 9.0 cm).**

$$R_{f,3} = \frac{9.0}{15.0}$$

$$R_{f,3} = 0.60$$

**Step (vi): Match with reference Rf table (from Section 2.4).**

| Spot | Experimental Rf | Closest Reference | Identity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.26 | Glycine (0.26) | **Glycine** |
| 2 | 0.38 | Alanine (0.38) | **Alanine** |
| 3 | 0.60 | Valine (0.60) | **Valine** |

**Step (vii): State the conclusion.**

> Therefore, the amino acid mixture contains **Glycine, Alanine, and Valine**, confirmed by their experimental Rf values matching the literature values within acceptable experimental error.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "What If" Troubleshooting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU lab record evaluation often includes troubleshooting viva. Use this matrix.

| Observation | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| Solvent front runs unevenly / curves | Chamber not saturated | Re-equilibrate chamber for 30 min |
| Spots are streaked (tailing) | Overloading of sample | Dilute sample; re-spot with smaller volume |
| Spots show no color after ninhydrin | Insufficient heating / old ninhydrin | Re-spray with fresh 0.2 % ninhydrin; heat at 105 °C |
| Spots migrate with the front ($R_f \to 1$) | Solvent too non-polar | Reduce butanol fraction, increase acetic acid/water |
| Spots do not move ($R_f \to 0$) | Solvent too polar / origin submerged | Increase organic fraction; ensure origin is above solvent |
| Multiple spurious spots appear | Contaminated glassware / fingers | Re-run using fresh solvents and gloves |
| Two amino acids merge into one spot | Inadequate solvent system | Switch to a second dimension or alternative solvent |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Experimental Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Reagent and Apparatus Setup] --> B[Cut Whatman No.1 Paper 15x20 cm]
    B --> C[Draw Pencil Origin Line 2 cm from Bottom]
    C --> D[Mark 3 to 4 Spotting Positions 1.5 cm Apart]
    D --> E[Spot Standard Amino Acid 1 on Position 1]
    E --> F[Spot Standard Amino Acid 2 on Position 2]
    F --> G[Spot Unknown Mixture on Position 3]
    G --> H[Air Dry Each Spot Between Applications]
    H --> I[Prepare BAW Solvent 4:1:1 in Chamber]
    I --> J[Line Chamber Walls with Solvent-Soaked Wicks]
    J --> K[Seal Lid and Equilibrate 20 to 30 Minutes]
    K --> L[Insert Paper with Origin Above Solvent Level]
    L --> M[Allow Ascending Solvent Flow for 90 to 120 Minutes]
    M --> N[Remove Paper and Mark Solvent Front Immediately]
    N --> O[Dry Paper in Fume Hood at 60 Degree Celsius]
    O --> P[Spray with 0.2 Percent Ninhydrin in Acetone]
    P --> Q[Heat at 100 to 110 Degree Celsius for 5 to 10 Minutes]
    Q --> R[Visible Purple Spots Appear Ruhemanns Purple]
    R --> S[Measure Distance Solute and Distance Solvent]
    S --> T[Calculate Rf using Rf equals d solute over d solvent]
    T --> U[Compare with Standard Reference Rf Table]
    U --> V[Identify Amino Acids in Unknown Mixture]
    V --> W[End Report Submission]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — The Chromatographic Separation Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Stationary Phase: Water trapped in hydrated cellulose fibers of Whatman No.1 paper] -->|Polar amino acids like Glycine and Lysine bind here| RESULT[Slower Migration Lower Rf]
    MP[Mobile Phase: n-Butanol-Acetic Acid-Water 4:1:1 ascending by capillary action] -->|Less polar amino acids like Leucine and Valine dissolve here| RESULT2[Faster Migration Higher Rf]
    SP --> RESULT2
    MP --> RESULT
    RESULT --> OUTCOME[Discrete Purple Spots on Developed Chromatogram]
    RESULT2 --> OUTCOME
    OUTCOME --> NINH[Ninhydrin Visualization at 105 Degree Celsius]
    NINH --> RFM[Measurement of Rf equals d solute divided by d solvent]
    RFM --> ID[Identification by Comparison with Standards]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rf Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ORIGIN[Mark Origin with Pencil Line] --> APPLYSAMPLE[Apply Amino Acid Sample as Small Spot]
    APPLYSAMPLE --> DRY[Air Dry the Spot Completely]
    DRY --> DEVELOP[Develop Chromatogram in BAW Solvent]
    DEVELOP --> MARKFRONT[Mark Solvent Front Position d_solvent]
    MARKFRONT --> SPRAY[Spray with Ninhydrin Reagent]
    SPRAY --> HEAT[Heat to 105 Degree Celsius for 10 Minutes]
    HEAT --> MEASURE[Measure Distance from Origin to Spot Center d_solute]
    MEASURE --> COMPUTE[Compute Rf using d_solute divided by d_solvent]
    COMPUTE --> TABLE[Match Against Standard Reference Rf Table]
    TABLE --> REPORT[Report Identity of Amino Acid with Solvent and Temperature Conditions]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A expects crisp, definition-style answers of 50–80 words each. Do not exceed 4–5 lines.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Define Retention factor (Rf). State its significance in paper chromatography.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Retention factor (Rf)** is defined as the ratio of the distance travelled by the substance (solute) from the origin to the distance travelled by the solvent front from the origin, both measured along the direction of solvent flow.
>
> $$R_f = \frac{d_{\text{solute}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}}$$
>
> It is a **dimensionless constant** (0 ≤ Rf ≤ 1) that characterizes the migration behaviour of a given substance under fixed chromatographic conditions. Its significance lies in **identification** — by comparing the experimental Rf of an unknown amino acid with that of a standard run under identical conditions, the unknown can be conclusively identified.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark significance.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Why is ninhydrin used as a visualizing agent for amino acids in paper chromatography?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Ninhydrin (2,2-dihydroxyindane-1,3-dione) is used because it **reacts with the free α-amino group of amino acids** to produce a coloured compound called **Ruhemann's Purple** (max absorbance ~ 570 nm). This reaction is highly sensitive, detecting as little as **1 µg** of amino acid.
>
> For **imino acids** like proline and hydroxyproline, ninhydrin produces a characteristic **yellow-brown** colour instead, which still allows detection. The reagent is typically prepared as a **0.2 % w/v solution in acetone** and applied after solvent development, followed by heating at **100–110 °C for 5–10 minutes** to develop the colour.

*(Mark split: 1 mark reagent identity, 1 mark chemistry, 1 mark application.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B Module questions carry **14 marks**, generally split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between two full questions. Choose **either Option A or Option B** in the exam hall.

---

### **🔹 OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **principle, procedure, and detection method** for the separation of amino acids by ascending paper chromatography using the BAW (4:1:1) solvent system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a paper chromatography experiment, the solvent front migrated **14.0 cm** and three amino acids gave spots at **3.5 cm, 6.3 cm, and 10.5 cm**. Calculate their Rf values and identify each amino acid using the following reference data: Glycine (0.26), Alanine (0.38), Valine (0.60), Leucine (0.73). **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle (2 marks):** Paper chromatography is a type of partition chromatography in which the stationary phase is the **water trapped within the hydrated cellulose matrix** of the filter paper, and the mobile phase is the **organic solvent system** (BAW 4:1:1). Separation is governed by the **differential partition coefficients** of amino acids between these two phases, as described by **Nernst's Distribution Law**.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Cut Whatman No. 1 paper into 15 × 20 cm strips; draw a pencil origin line 2 cm from the bottom edge.
2. Spot 2–3 µL of each amino acid standard and the unknown on marked positions; dry between applications.
3. Place BAW (4:1:1) solvent in the chromatographic chamber; saturate the chamber atmosphere for 20–30 minutes.
4. Suspend the spotted paper in the chamber ensuring the origin is above the solvent level; develop until the solvent front reaches ~3 cm from the top (~ 90–120 min).
5. Remove the paper, mark the solvent front, dry, and spray with 0.2 % ninhydrin solution.

**Detection (2 marks):** Heat the sprayed paper at **100–110 °C for 5–10 minutes** in an oven. Amino acids appear as **purple spots** (Ruhemann's Purple) against a pale background; imino acids (proline, hydroxyproline) appear **yellow-brown**. Measure the distance of each spot centre from the origin and compute Rf.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given data:**

$$d_{\text{solvent}} = 14.0 \; \text{cm}$$

**Spot 1 calculation:**

$$R_{f,1} = \frac{3.5}{14.0} = 0.25$$

**Spot 2 calculation:**

$$R_{f,2} = \frac{6.3}{14.0} = 0.45$$

**Spot 3 calculation:**

$$R_{f,3} = \frac{10.5}{14.0} = 0.75$$

**Identification (matching within ± 0.05 tolerance):**

| Spot | Experimental Rf | Closest Reference | Identity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.25 | Glycine (0.26) | **Glycine** |
| 2 | 0.45 | — (no close match) | Not in given list |
| 3 | 0.75 | Leucine (0.73) | **Leucine** |

**Conclusion:** Spot 1 is **Glycine**, Spot 3 is **Leucine**. Spot 2 (Rf = 0.45) does not match any amino acid in the provided reference list — it may be **proline** (literature Rf ≈ 0.43 in BAW).

*(Mark split for part b: 1 mark formula, 3 marks calculation, 2 marks identification, 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

### **🔹 OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **theory of partition chromatography** and discuss the **factors affecting Rf values** in paper chromatography. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of the solvent system, chamber saturation, and origin-line position** in obtaining reproducible Rf values. Describe **two alternative solvent systems** to BAW used for amino acid separation. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Theory of Partition Chromatography (4 marks):**

Paper chromatography operates on the principle of **partition chromatography** developed by **Martin and Synge (1941)**, who later received the Nobel Prize for their work on partition chromatography.

The two phases are:
- **Stationary phase:** Water molecules hydrogen-bonded to the cellulose hydroxyl groups of the filter paper.
- **Mobile phase:** An organic solvent (or solvent mixture) that migrates through the paper by **capillary action**.

When a mixture of amino acids is spotted on the origin and developed, each amino acid distributes itself between the stationary and mobile phases in accordance with its **partition coefficient (K)**:

$$K = \frac{C_{\text{stationary}}}{C_{\text{mobile}}}$$

Amino acids with **higher K** (more polar, e.g., lysine, arginine) prefer the aqueous stationary phase and **migrate slowly → low Rf**. Amino acids with **lower K** (less polar, e.g., leucine, valine) prefer the mobile phase and **migrate faster → high Rf**. This differential migration results in **spatial separation** of the components.

**Factors Affecting Rf (3 marks):**
1. **Nature and composition of the solvent system** (polarity, pH).
2. **Temperature** of the chamber during development.
3. **Saturation of the chamber** with solvent vapour.
4. **Distance of the origin from solvent surface** (must be ≥ 1 cm).
5. **Quality of the chromatography paper** (uniform capillary flow).
6. **Distance over which the solvent front travels**.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Role of Solvent System (2 marks):** The solvent system determines the **polarity and pH** of the mobile phase. In BAW, **acetic acid suppresses ionization** of amino acid functional groups (–COOH and –NH₃⁺), minimizing tailing and ensuring reproducible migration. A solvent that is too polar gives $R_f \to 0$; too non-polar gives $R_f \to 1$. The **4:1:1 ratio** of BAW is empirically optimized for most standard amino acids.

**Role of Chamber Saturation (2 marks):** If the chamber is not saturated with solvent vapour, **evaporation** occurs from the paper surface as the solvent ascends, causing the solvent front to advance unevenly and Rf values to be irreproducible. A **20–30 minute pre-saturation** with solvent-soaked wicks lining the chamber walls establishes equilibrium between liquid and vapour phases, ensuring **uniform solvent flow** and **reliable Rf values**.

**Role of Origin-Line Position (1 mark):** The origin line must be drawn **at least 1–2 cm above the solvent surface**; if it dips into the solvent, the sample **dissolves directly into the reservoir** rather than being carried up by capillary flow, ruining the chromatogram.

**Two Alternative Solvent Systems (2 marks):**
1. **Phenol : Water (4 : 1 w/v)** — used for separating polar amino acids (aspartate, glutamate, serine) and for 2D chromatography in combination with BAW.
2. **n-Butanol : Pyridine : Water (6 : 4 : 3 v/v)** — an excellent alternative for amino acids that co-migrate in BAW; the basic pyridine alters the ionization state and gives different Rf values, enabling orthogonal identification.

*(Total marks split: clearly demarcate with bold subsection labels.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common places where KTU students LOSE MARKS in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the solvent system when quoting an Rf value** — Examiner deducts 1–2 marks instantly. Always write: *“Rf = 0.38 in BAW (4:1:1) at 25 °C”*.
>
> 2. **Marking the solvent front AFTER it has dried** — Solvent fronts evaporate within seconds of removing the paper. Mark it **immediately** with a pencil while still wet. Failure to do so invalidates the Rf calculation.
>
> 3. **Touching the paper with bare fingers** — Skin oils and amino acids from sweat contaminate the chromatogram, producing spurious spots. Always use **gloves or forceps**.
>
> 4. **Submerging the origin line in the solvent** — The sample dissolves into the reservoir. Origin must be **≥ 2 cm above** solvent surface.
>
> 5. **Writing the answer in passive tense without experimental details** — KTU lab record marks require **observation, inference, and result** in tabular form. A bare calculation without specifying the solvent system, temperature, and paper grade earns only partial credit.
>
> 6. **Confusing Ninhydrin reaction products** — Most amino acids → **purple** (Ruhemann's Purple); proline/hydroxyproline → **yellow-brown**. Examiners love this distinction.
>
> 7. **Quoting Rf > 1 or Rf < 0** — Indicates measurement error. Re-check ruler placement and pencil markings.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [x] Paper chromatography is a **partition technique** (Martin & Synge, 1941).
- [x] **Stationary phase** = water in hydrated cellulose fibers.
- [x] **Mobile phase** = organic solvent (BAW 4:1:1 standard).
- [x] **Rf is dimensionless**; always between 0 and 1.
- [x] Rf is **NOT a universal constant** — it depends on solvent, paper, and temperature.

### 🧪 Experiment Essentials
- [x] **Paper:** Whatman No. 1.
- [x] **Solvent:** n-Butanol : Acetic Acid : Water = 4 : 1 : 1 (upper layer used).
- [x] **Origin:** Drawn in **pencil** (never pen — ink dissolves), **2 cm** above bottom edge.
- [x] **Spotting:** 2–3 µL, dry between applications, repeat 3 times.
- [x] **Chamber saturation:** ≥ 20–30 minutes.
- [x] **Development time:** 90–120 minutes.
- [x] **Detection:** 0.2 % ninhydrin, heat at 100–110 °C for 5–10 min.
- [x] **Mark solvent front immediately** after removing the paper.

### 🧠 Detection Chemistry
- [x] Ninhydrin + α-amino acid → **Ruhemann's Purple** (570 nm).
- [x] Proline / hydroxyproline → **yellow-brown** (yellow colour exception).
- [x] Detection limit ~ **1 µg** amino acid.

### 📐 Critical Formulas
- [x] $R_f = \dfrac{d_{\text{solute}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}}$
- [x] $K = \dfrac{C_{\text{stationary}}}{C_{\text{mobile}}}$

### ⚠️ Pitfalls to Avoid
- [x] Don't forget to state the **solvent system and temperature** with every Rf value.
- [x] Don't touch the paper with bare fingers.
- [x] Don't let the origin line dip into the solvent reservoir.
- [x] Don't quote Rf > 1 (calculation/measurement error).
- [x] Don't forget to dry the chamber atmosphere with wicks for 20 min.

### 🆎 Two-Stage Identification
- [x] Compare unknown Rf with **standards run on the SAME paper** (most reliable).
- [x] If no match, cross-check with **alternative solvent system** or **2D chromatography**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thin Layer Chromatography – Extraction of Lipids and Separation Using Thin Layer Chromatography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL307 - BIOTECHNOLOGY ENGINEERING (BT) BRANCHES)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Thin Layer Chromatography – Extraction of Lipids and Separation Using Thin Layer Chromatography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Lipids?

**Lipids** are a diverse group of naturally occurring hydrophobic or amphipathic small molecules that are soluble in non-polar organic solvents (such as chloroform, ether, and benzene) but poorly soluble in water. Chemically, they include fatty acids, triglycerides, phospholipids, sterols, sphingolipids, waxes, and fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBBL307):** Lipids are broadly classified into **three major classes** relevant to TLC separation:
> 1. **Neutral Lipids** – Mono-, di-, and triacylglycerols, cholesterol, cholesteryl esters, free fatty acids.
> 2. **Phospholipids (Polar Lipids)** – Phosphatidylcholine (PC), phosphatidylethanolamine (PE), phosphatidylserine (PS), phosphatidylinositol (PI).
> 3. **Glycolipids** – Cerebrosides, gangliosides, sulfatides.

## 1.2 What is Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC)?

**Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC)** is a planar chromatographic separation technique in which a thin uniform layer of stationary phase (typically silica gel $\text{SiO}_2 \cdot \text{xH}_2\text{O}$ or alumina, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) coated on a glass, aluminum, or plastic plate serves as the support, and a liquid mobile phase (solvent or solvent mixture) migrates through the stationary phase by capillary action, separating the components of a mixture based on their differential **adsorption** (or **partition**) affinities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Module 2 Experiment):** TLC is a simple, rapid, inexpensive, and qualitative/semi-quantitative analytical method used to identify lipid classes by comparing the **Retention factor ($R_f$)** of unknown samples with that of authentic lipid standards run under identical solvent and temperature conditions.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **marble race track** where different marbles (lipid molecules) are dropped at the starting line:
- The **track surface** (silica gel) is slightly sticky — but some marbles stick more than others.
- A gentle **breeze of oil** (mobile phase solvent) blows across the track, pushing the marbles forward.
- **Sticky marbles** (polar lipids like phospholipids) cling to the track and barely move — they finish last.
- **Slippery marbles** (non-polar lipids like triglycerides) slide easily along the oil-slicked track and finish first.
- The **distance each marble travels** relative to the solvent front is its "**score**" — the **$R_f$ value** in chromatography.

Just as you can identify a marble by how far it travels on a known track, TLC lets you identify a lipid by its characteristic **$R_f$** under standard conditions.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard TLC plate dimensions:** **$20 \text{ cm} \times 20 \text{ cm}$** (or $5 \text{ cm} \times 10 \text{ cm}$ for analytical micro-plates).
- **Standard stationary phase thickness:** **$250 \text{ \mu m}$** (analytical) or **$500\text{–}2000 \text{ \mu m}$** (preparative).
- **Typical sample volume loaded:** **$1\text{–}10 \text{ \mu L}$** using a micropipette or microcapillary.
- **Solvent front migration distance:** **$10 \text{ cm}$** (standard development).
- **Operating temperature:** Ambient (**$20\text{–}25 \text{ °C}$**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal TLC Rule:** The $R_f$ value is **dimensionless** and always lies between **$0$** (component does not migrate, remains at origin) and **$1$** (component migrates with the solvent front). For lipid TLC, optimal $R_f$ values lie between **$0.3$ and $0.7$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Principle of TLC

TLC operates on the principle of **differential adsorption equilibrium** between two immiscible phases:

1. **Stationary Phase (S):** A polar adsorbent (silica gel or alumina) is fixed on the plate. The surface silanol groups ($\text{Si-OH}$) form **hydrogen bonds** with polar analytes, retarding their movement.
2. **Mobile Phase (M):** A non-polar (or moderately polar) solvent or solvent mixture moves up the plate by **capillary action**. It carries the analytes with it.
3. **Distribution Equilibrium:** Each analyte distributes itself between S and M, characterized by a **partition coefficient** $K$.

The governing equation is:

$$K = \frac{C_S}{C_M}$$

where $C_S$ = concentration of analyte in the stationary phase, and $C_M$ = concentration of analyte in the mobile phase.

- A **highly polar lipid** (e.g., phosphatidylcholine) has a large $K$ → binds strongly to silica → moves **slowly** → low $R_f$.
- A **non-polar lipid** (e.g., triglyceride) has a small $K$ → prefers mobile phase → moves **fast** → high $R_f$.

## 2.2 The Five Operational Steps of TLC for Lipids

| Step | Operation | KTU-Exam-Relevant Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Plate Preparation** | Cut plate; mark origin line **$1.5 \text{ cm}$** from bottom and solvent front limit **$1 \text{ cm}$** from top using a pencil (NEVER pen — ink dissolves in solvent). | Use **pencil** only; ballpoint pen pigments co-migrate and confuse the chromatogram. |
| **2. Sample Application** | Dissolve lipid extract in **chloroform** ($\text{CHCl}_3$) at **$1\text{–}10 \text{ mg/mL}$**; spot **$2\text{–}5 \text{ \mu L}$** gently using a microcapillary. | Spots should be **$< 2 \text{ mm}$** diameter to avoid band-broadening (tailing). |
| **3. Chamber Saturation** | Place plate in a closed TLC chamber pre-saturated with mobile phase vapor for **$10\text{–}15 \text{ min}$**. | Saturation ensures uniform solvent flow and reproducible $R_f$ values. |
| **4. Development** | Allow solvent to ascend by capillary action until the front reaches the upper limit line. | Typical development time: **$30\text{–}45 \text{ min}$**. |
| **5. Visualization & Analysis** | Dry plate; visualize spots using **UV light ($254 \text{ nm}$)** for UV-active lipids, or spray with **destructive stains** (e.g., **$50\%$ \, H_2\text{SO}_4$ followed by charring at $120 \text{ °C}$** for 15 min) or **non-destructive stains** (e.g., **iodine vapor**, **$0.2\%$ 2,7-dichlorofluorescein**). | For phospholipids specifically, use **molybdenum blue spray** (Dittmer-Lester reagent) — gives a characteristic **blue** spot. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_f = \dfrac{d_{\text{substance}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}}$ | **Retention factor** — distance traveled by substance divided by distance traveled by solvent front. | Dimensionless; $0 \leq R_f \leq 1$ |
| 2 | $R_f = \dfrac{1}{1 + K \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_S}{V_M}\right)}$ | Thermodynamic form of $R_f$ relating it to partition coefficient $K$ and phase volume ratio. | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $d_{\text{substance}}$ | Migration distance of analyte spot from origin (NOT center of spot — use leading edge for accuracy). | cm or mm |
| 4 | $d_{\text{solvent}}$ | Migration distance of solvent front from origin. | cm or mm |
| 5 | $\alpha = \dfrac{R_{f2}}{R_{f1}}$ (with $R_{f2} > R_{f1}$) | **Selectivity factor** between two adjacent lipid classes. | $\alpha \neq 1$ for separation |
| 6 | $N = 16 \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{w}\right)^2$ | **Theoretical plates** ($N$) — measure of column efficiency; $d$ = migration distance, $w$ = spot width. | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Folch ratio: $\text{CHCl}_3 : \text{CH}_3\text{OH} = 2 : 1 \, (v/v)$ | Standard Folch extraction solvent system for total lipid extraction. | Volumetric ratio |
| 8 | Bligh & Dyer ratio: $\text{CHCl}_3 : \text{CH}_3\text{OH} : \text{H}_2\text{O} = 2 : 2 : 1.8$ | Modified Folch for tissues with high water content. | Volumetric ratio |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Industrial Utility

TLC-based lipid separation is foundational in:

- **Food industry:** Quality control of edible oils (detecting adulteration, oxidation products).
- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Purity testing of lipid-based drug carriers (liposomes, solid lipid nanoparticles).
- **Cosmetics:** Validation of lipid content in moisturizers and emulsions.
- **Clinical diagnostics:** Diagnosing lipid-storage diseases (e.g., **Gaucher's disease**, **Niemann-Pick disease**) via abnormal lipid profiles in patient blood/urine.
- **Forensic science:** Identifying lipids in crime-scene residues (oils, fats).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Retention Factor ($R_f$)

### Background

A lipid molecule spotted on a TLC plate is constantly partitioning between the **stationary phase** (silica gel surface) and the **mobile phase** (solvent). We want a mathematical expression for the fraction of time the molecule spends in the mobile phase (since only there does it move forward).

### Step 1: Define the time fractions

Let $t_S$ = time spent in stationary phase, $t_M$ = time spent in mobile phase, and $T = t_S + t_M$ = total time.

The fraction of time in the mobile phase:

$$f_M = \frac{t_M}{T}$$

The fraction of time in the stationary phase:

$$f_S = \frac{t_S}{T} = 1 - f_M$$

### Step 2: Relate $f_M$ to phase volumes and concentrations

The number of molecules in each phase is proportional to concentration $\times$ volume:

$$\frac{N_M}{N_S} = \frac{C_M \cdot V_M}{C_S \cdot V_S} = \frac{V_M}{K \cdot V_S}$$

Therefore:

$$f_M = \frac{N_M}{N_M + N_S} = \frac{C_M V_M}{C_M V_M + C_S V_S}$$

### Step 3: Express in terms of $K$

Substituting $C_S = K \cdot C_M$:

$$f_M = \frac{C_M V_M}{C_M V_M + K C_M V_S} = \frac{V_M}{V_M + K V_S}$$

### Step 4: Link $f_M$ to distance traveled

In time $T$, the solvent front travels distance $d_{\text{solvent}} = v_M \cdot T$ (where $v_M$ is mobile phase linear velocity). The lipid only moves during the fraction $f_M$ of this time, so:

$$d_{\text{substance}} = v_M \cdot f_M \cdot T = f_M \cdot d_{\text{solvent}}$$

### Step 5: Combine to obtain $R_f$

$$R_f = \frac{d_{\text{substance}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}} = f_M = \frac{V_M}{V_M + K \cdot V_S}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{R_f = \frac{1}{1 + K \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_S}{V_M}\right)}}$$

### Step 6: Interpret limiting cases

- If $K \to 0$ (analyte has zero affinity for stationary phase) → $R_f \to 1$ (analyte runs with solvent front — e.g., squalene).
- If $K \to \infty$ (analyte binds infinitely to stationary phase) → $R_f \to 0$ (analyte stays at origin — e.g., very polar glycolipid).
- For typical lipid separations, $V_S / V_M$ is constant for a given plate type, so **$R_f$ depends only on $K$**, which is governed by solvent polarity and analyte polarity.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** A lipid extract was spotted on a silica gel TLC plate and developed in a chloroform:methanol:water ($65:25:4$, $v/v/v$) solvent system. After development, the solvent front traveled **$9.5 \text{ cm}$** from the origin. A standard **phosphatidylcholine (PC)** spot traveled **$3.8 \text{ cm}$** from the origin, and an unknown lipid spot traveled **$5.7 \text{ cm}$**. Calculate the $R_f$ values and identify the unknown.

### Solution

**Step 1: Calculate $R_f$ of PC standard**

$$R_f^{\text{PC}} = \frac{d_{\text{PC}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}} = \frac{3.8 \text{ cm}}{9.5 \text{ cm}} = 0.40$$

**Step 2: Calculate $R_f$ of unknown**

$$R_f^{\text{unknown}} = \frac{d_{\text{unknown}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}} = \frac{5.7 \text{ cm}}{9.5 \text{ cm}} = 0.60$$

**Step 3: Compare with reference table**

In a $\text{CHCl}_3 : \text{CH}_3\text{OH} : \text{H}_2\text{O} \, (65:25:4)$ solvent system, the reference $R_f$ values on silica gel are:

| Lipid Class | Approximate $R_f$ |
|---|---|
| Phosphatidylcholine (PC) | $0.40$ |
| Phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) | $0.55$ |
| Phosphatidylserine (PS) | $0.30$ |
| Phosphatidylinositol (PI) | $0.20$ |
| Sphingomyelin | $0.35$ |
| Free fatty acid | $0.75$ |
| Triglyceride | $0.90$ |
| Cholesterol | $0.65$ |

**Step 4: Match the unknown**

The unknown's $R_f = 0.60$ is closest to **cholesterol** ($R_f = 0.65$) but more likely corresponds to **phosphatidylethanolamine (PE)** under slightly different lab conditions. The student should state:

> *"The unknown lipid has $R_f = 0.60$, which closely matches the standard $R_f$ of **phosphatidylethanolamine (PE)**, suggesting the unknown is PE. Confirmation requires co-chromatography with an authentic PE standard."*

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Solvent System for Lipid TLC

| Polarity of Target Lipid | Recommended Solvent System | Expected $R_f$ Range |
|---|---|---|
| Highly polar (phospholipids, glycolipids) | $\text{CHCl}_3 : \text{CH}_3\text{OH} : \text{H}_2\text{O} \, (65:25:4)$ | $0.2 - 0.5$ |
| Moderately polar (free fatty acids, monoacylglycerols) | $\text{Petroleum ether : Diethyl ether : Acetic acid} \, (80:20:1)$ | $0.3 - 0.6$ |
| Non-polar (triglycerides, cholesteryl esters, squalene) | $\text{Hexane : Diethyl ether : Acetic acid} \, (90:10:1)$ | $0.5 - 0.95$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of TLC Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Sample Preparation] --> A1[Homogenize tissue with Folch solvent CHCl3:CH3OH 2:1]
    A1 --> A2[Filter and partition with 0.9 percent NaCl wash]
    A2 --> A3[Collect organic phase and evaporate to dryness]
    A3 --> A4[Resuspend lipid pellet in chloroform at 10 mg per mL]

    A4 --> B[Step 2: TLC Plate Activation]
    B --> B1[Mark origin line 1.5 cm from bottom with pencil]
    B1 --> B2[Activate plate at 110 degrees C for 30 min in oven]
    B2 --> B3[Cool to room temperature in desiccator]

    B3 --> C[Step 3: Sample Spotting]
    C --> C1[Load 2 to 5 microliter of lipid extract using microcapillary]
    C1 --> C2[Spot authentic lipid standards on adjacent lanes]
    C2 --> C3[Allow spots to dry completely]

    C3 --> D[Step 4: Chamber Saturation]
    D --> D1[Pour mobile phase into TLC chamber]
    D1 --> D2[Line chamber with filter paper wick for vapor saturation]
    D2 --> D3[Wait 15 min for vapor equilibrium]

    D3 --> E[Step 5: Development]
    E --> E1[Carefully place plate in chamber touching solvent]
    E1 --> E2[Seal chamber and allow solvent to ascend]
    E2 --> E3[Remove when solvent front reaches 1 cm below top edge]

    E3 --> F[Step 6: Visualization]
    F --> F1[Mark solvent front immediately with pencil]
    F1 --> F2[Dry plate in fume hood]
    F2 --> F3[Visualize under UV 254 nm for UV active lipids]
    F3 --> F4[Spray with 50 percent H2SO4 and char at 120 degrees C for 15 min]
    F4 --> F5[Spray with molybdenum blue for phospholipid specific detection]

    F5 --> G[Step 7: Analysis]
    G --> G1[Measure distance of each spot from origin]
    G1 --> G2[Calculate Rf value for each spot]
    G2 --> G3[Compare with Rf of authentic standards]
    G3 --> G4[Identify lipid classes in unknown sample]
```

## 4.3 Lipid Extraction — Block Flow of Folch Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Tissue Sample 1 g] --> T2[Homogenize in 20 mL CHCl3:CH3OH 2:1]
    T2 --> T3[Add 5 mL 0.9 percent NaCl]

    T4 --> T5[Two phases form: upper aqueous and lower organic]
    T5 --> T6[Discard upper aqueous phase]
```
