# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024 Scheme]`

**Define Thin Layer Chromatography. What is the principle on which separation of lipids by TLC is based?**

**Model Answer:**

Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) is a planar chromatographic technique in which a thin layer of stationary phase (e.g., silica gel) coated on a glass or aluminum plate is used to separate mixtures of compounds by differential migration in a mobile phase (solvent or solvent mixture).

The principle is based on the **differential adsorption** of lipid components between the polar stationary phase (silica gel, with surface $\text{Si-OH}$ groups) and the mobile organic phase. Polar lipids (e.g., phospholipids) form hydrogen bonds with silica and migrate slowly, giving low $R_f$ values. Non-polar lipids (e.g., triglycerides, cholesteryl esters) interact weakly with silica, dissolve preferentially in the non-polar mobile phase, and migrate quickly with high $R_f$ values.

**(Marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for principle)**

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024 Scheme]`

**Write the formula for the Retention factor ($R_f$) in TLC. What is the significance of the $R_f$ value in lipid identification?**

**Model Answer:**

The Retention factor is given by:

$$R_f = \frac{d_{\text{substance}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}}$$

where $d_{\text{substance}}$ is the distance traveled by the lipid spot from the origin, and $d_{\text{solvent}}$ is the distance traveled by the solvent front from the origin.

**Significance in lipid identification:**
- The $R_f$ value is a **physical constant** for a given lipid in a given solvent system on a given stationary phase.
- It is **dimensionless** and lies between 0 and 1.
- By comparing the $R_f$ of an unknown lipid spot with that of authentic lipid standards run under **identical** conditions, the lipid class can be identified.
- This is the basis of qualitative lipid analysis by TLC.

**(Marks: 1 for formula + 2 for significance)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B questions carry 14 marks each, typically divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternative choices are given below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Folch method** for the extraction of total lipids from a biological tissue. Include the solvent composition, procedural steps, and the underlying principle of phase separation.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain the procedure for separation of lipid classes by Thin Layer Chromatography, including (i) stationary and mobile phase composition, (ii) sample application technique, and (iii) three common methods of visualization of lipid spots.

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Principle:** The Folch method (Folch, Lees & Sloane-Stanley, 1957) uses a mixture of chloroform (non-polar) and methanol (polar) to disrupt cell membranes and solubilize both polar and non-polar lipids. Subsequent addition of water/saline induces a biphasic system where lipids partition into the lower organic chloroform layer, while hydrophilic contaminants (sugars, amino acids, salts) partition into the upper aqueous methanol-water layer.

**Solvent Composition:** Chloroform : Methanol in the ratio **$2 : 1$ (v/v)**.

**Procedure (7 steps):**
1. Weigh **$1 \text{ g}$** of fresh tissue; cut into small pieces.
2. Homogenize in **$20 \text{ mL}$** of $\text{CHCl}_3 : \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \, (2:1, v/v)$ using a tissue homogenizer.
3. Filter the homogenate through cheesecloth or Whatman No. 1 filter paper.
4. Add **$0.2$ volume** of **$0.9\% \, \text{NaCl}$** solution (e.g., $4 \text{ mL}$ for $20 \text{ mL}$ filtrate) to induce phase separation.
5. Vortex vigorously for **$1 \text{ min}$** and allow to stand or centrifuge at **$2000 \text{ rpm}$ for $10 \text{ min}$**.
6. Two distinct phases form: **upper aqueous-methanol phase** (discard) and **lower chloroform phase** (contains total lipids).
7. Carefully collect the lower organic phase using a Pasteur pipette; evaporate solvent under nitrogen stream or rotary evaporator; weigh the lipid extract.

**Yield Calculation:**

$$\text{Lipid yield (\%)} = \frac{\text{Weight of extracted lipid (g)}}{\text{Weight of tissue (g)}} \times 100$$

**(Marks: 1 for principle + 1 for solvent + 4 for procedure + 1 for yield formula)**

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Stationary and Mobile Phase (3 marks):**

- **Stationary phase:** Silica gel 60 (particle size **$5\text{–}20 \text{ \mu m}$**, pore size **$60 \text{ Å}$**, layer thickness **$250 \text{ \mu m}$**) on a glass or aluminum plate. The silanol ($\text{Si-OH}$) groups on silica provide hydrogen-bonding sites that adsorb polar analytes.
- **Mobile phase (solvent system):** Choice depends on lipid polarity.
  - For **neutral lipids:** $\text{Petroleum ether : Diethyl ether : Acetic acid} \, (80:20:1, v/v/v)$.
  - For **phospholipids:** $\text{CHCl}_3 : \text{CH}_3\text{OH} : \text{H}_2\text{O} \, (65:25:4, v/v/v)$.

**(ii) Sample Application (2 marks):**

- Dissolve lipid extract in chloroform at a concentration of **$1\text{–}10 \text{ mg/mL}$**.
- Using a **microcapillary tube** or micropipette, apply **$2\text{–}5 \text{ \mu L}$** of the lipid solution on the origin line (drawn **$1.5 \text{ cm}$** from the bottom edge with a sharp pencil).
- Maintain a minimum gap of **$1 \text{ cm}$** between adjacent spots.
- Allow the spot to dry completely before development. Spot diameter should not exceed **$2 \text{ mm}$** to avoid tailing.

**(iii) Three Visualization Methods (2 marks):**

1. **Iodine Vapor Staining** — Place the developed plate in a closed jar containing a few iodine crystals. Iodine vapors reversibly complex with unsaturated lipid double bonds, producing **yellow-brown spots**. Non-destructive; lipids can be recovered.
2. **$50\%$ Sulfuric Acid Spray and Charring** — Spray plate with $50\% \, \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, then heat at **$120 \text{ °C}$ for $15 \text{ min}$**. All lipid classes char to **black/dark brown spots**. Destructive.
3. **Molybdenum Blue Spray (Dittmer-Lester Reagent)** — Specific for phospholipids; produces **blue spots** on a white background. Non-destructive for further analysis.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the Retention factor ($R_f$) in TLC? Derive the expression for $R_f$ in terms of the partition coefficient $K$ and the phase volume ratio.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A lipid mixture containing triglyceride, cholesterol, free fatty acid, and phosphatidylcholine was separated by TLC on silica gel using a petroleum ether:diethyl ether:acetic acid ($80:20:1$) solvent system. The solvent front traveled **$10.0 \text{ cm}$**. The following migration distances were observed: triglyceride **$9.2 \text{ cm}$**, cholesterol **$6.5 \text{ cm}$**, free fatty acid **$4.0 \text{ cm}$**, and phosphatidylcholine **$1.0 \text{ cm}$**. Calculate the $R_f$ values and arrange the lipids in order of increasing polarity.

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Retention factor ($R_f$) is defined as the ratio of the distance traveled by the substance from the origin to the distance traveled by the solvent front from the origin:

$$R_f = \frac{d_{\text{substance}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}}$$

**Derivation (5 marks):** See the full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps:

1. Define $f_M$ = fraction of time analyte spends in mobile phase = $V_M / (V_M + K \cdot V_S)$.
2. Distance traveled by analyte = $f_M \times d_{\text{solvent}}$.
3. Therefore:

$$R_f = \frac{1}{1 + K \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_S}{V_M}\right)}$$

**Limiting cases:**
- $K \to 0 \implies R_f \to 1$ (non-polar analyte).
- $K \to \infty \implies R_f \to 0$ (very polar analyte).

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Apply $R_f$ formula for each lipid (4 marks)**

| Lipid | $d_{\text{substance}}$ (cm) | $R_f = d_{\text{substance}} / 10.0$ |
|---|---|---|
| Triglyceride (TG) | $9.2$ | $0.92$ |
| Cholesterol (CHOL) | $6.5$ | $0.65$ |
| Free fatty acid (FFA) | $4.0$ | $0.40$ |
| Phosphatidylcholine (PC) | $1.0$ | $0.10$ |

Detailed calculations:

$$R_f^{\text{TG}} = \frac{9.2}{10.0} = 0.92$$

$$R_f^{\text{CHOL}} = \frac{6.5}{10.0} = 0.65$$

$$R_f^{\text{FFA}} = \frac{4.0}{10.0} = 0.40$$

$$R_f^{\text{PC}} = \frac{1.0}{10.0} = 0.10$$

**Step 2: Arrange in order of increasing polarity (3 marks)**

Polarity is **inversely proportional** to $R_f$ in normal-phase TLC (silica = polar stationary phase). So order of increasing polarity = order of **decreasing** $R_f$:

$$\underbrace{\text{TG } (R_f = 0.92)}_{\text{Least polar}} \to \underbrace{\text{CHOL } (0.65)}_{\text{Moderately polar}} \to \underbrace{\text{FFA } (0.40)}_{\text{More polar}} \to \underbrace{\text{PC } (R_f = 0.10)}_{\text{Most polar}}$$

**Biological interpretation:** Triglycerides (pure hydrocarbon chains) are most non-polar and travel with the solvent front. Phosphatidylcholine has a polar phosphate head-group and a quaternary ammonium; it binds strongly to silica and barely migrates.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units of $d_{\text{solvent}}$ and $d_{\text{substance}}$** in numerical answers — they must be in the **same unit** (both cm or both mm). Marks lost: 0.5.
> 2. **Using ballpoint pen** for marking origin and solvent front lines — invisible to most scanners and not accepted by examiners. Always say **"pencil"**. Marks lost: 0.5–1.
> 3. **Confusing Folch ($2:1$) and Bligh-Dyer ($2:2:1.8$) ratios** — Folch is for tissues, Bligh-Dyer is for high-water-content samples. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 4. **Writing $R_f$ value $> 1$** — physically impossible. If your calculation gives $R_f > 1$, the spot has run with the solvent front; recalculate or check measurements. Marks lost: 1.
> 5. **Not stating that the chamber must be saturated with solvent vapor** before development — examiners look for this; without saturation, $R_f$ values are non-reproducible. Marks lost: 1.
> 6. **Misidentifying lipid classes in the order-of-polarity question** — Phospholipids (PC, PE) are MORE polar than cholesterol and FFA; triglycerides are LEAST polar. Marks lost: 2.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **TLC Principle:** Differential adsorption between polar stationary phase (silica/alumina) and mobile organic phase; polar lipids stay back, non-polar lipids move forward.
- [ ] **Standard Plate Size:** $20 \text{ cm} \times 20 \text{ cm}$; **layer thickness** $250 \text{ \mu m}$ (analytical) or $500\text{–}2000 \text{ \mu m}$ (preparative).
- [ ] **Origin Line:** Drawn **$1.5 \text{ cm}$** from the bottom with a **pencil** (NEVER pen).
- [ ] **Solvent Front Limit:** **$1 \text{ cm}$** from top edge; standard development distance is **$10 \text{ cm}$**.
- [ ] **Sample Volume:** **$2\text{–}5 \text{ \mu L}$** of **$1\text{–}10 \text{ mg/mL}$** lipid solution in chloroform.
- [ ] **Spot Diameter:** Should be **$< 2 \text{ mm}$** to avoid band-broadening.
- [ ] **Chamber Saturation Time:** **$10\text{–}15 \text{ min}$** before development for reproducible $R_f$.
- [ ] **R_f Formula:** $R_f = d_{\text{substance}} / d_{\text{solvent}}$; dimensionless, $0 \leq R_f \leq 1$.
- [ ] **Optimal R_f Range for Lipids:** $0.3 - 0.7$.
- [ ] **Folch Solvent:** $\text{CHCl}_3 : \text{CH}_3\text{OH} = 2 : 1 (v/v)$; Bligh-Dyer: $2 : 2 : 1.8$ (with water).
- [ ] **Solvent System for Neutral Lipids:** $\text{Pet ether : Diethyl ether : AcOH} = 80 : 20 : 1$.
- [ ] **Solvent System for Polar Lipids:** $\text{CHCl}_3 : \text{CH}_3\text{OH} : \text{H}_2\text{O} = 65 : 25 : 4$.
- [ ] **Visualization — Universal:** $50\% \, \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ + char at $120 \text{ °C}$ for $15 \text{ min}$ → black spots.
- [ ] **Visualization — Iodine Vapor:** Yellow-brown spots; non-destructive; for unsaturated lipids.
- [ ] **Visualization — Molybdenum Blue:** Specific for phospholipids → blue spots.
- [ ] **Visualization — UV 254 nm:** For UV-active lipids (e.g., cholesterol, conjugated dienes).
- [ ] **Order of Polarity (Least → Most):** Triglycerides → Cholesteryl esters → Cholesterol → Free fatty acids → Mono-, di-acylglycerols → Phosphatidylcholine → Phosphatidylethanolamine → Phosphatidylserine → Phosphatidylinositol → Glycolipids.
- [ ] **R_f is Inversely Proportional to Polarity** in normal-phase TLC.
- [ ] **Co-chromatography:** Definitive identification requires running the unknown alongside an authentic standard under identical conditions.

### 🧠 Memory Hook

> **"Polar Probes Ponder, Non-Polar Plays":** On a polar silica plate, **polar lipids** **Ponder** (move slowly, low $R_f$); **non-polar lipids** **Play** (move freely with solvent, high $R_f$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Column Chromatography – Determination of Molecular Weight of Macromolecules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL307 - BIOTECHNOLOGY ENGINEERING (BT) BRANCHES)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Column Chromatography – Determination of Molecular Weight of Macromolecules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Column Chromatography for Molecular Weight Determination** is a laboratory technique that employs **Gel Filtration Chromatography (GFC)**, also known as **Size Exclusion Chromatography (SEC)**, to separate macromolecules (proteins, polysaccharides, nucleic acids) based on their hydrodynamic size and shape, thereby enabling the determination of their **molecular weight (MW)** through comparison with calibrated standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment uses a packed column containing porous beads of a specific gel matrix. Molecules are fractionated according to their ability to penetrate the internal pore network of the beads — large molecules elute first, small molecules elute last.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Molecular Sieve" Concept

Imagine a **basket of mixed marbles** (large, medium, small) being poured into a vertical pipe filled with **sponge balls** that have holes of a fixed size:

- **Large marbles** cannot enter the sponges → they roll down the outside quickly and exit first.
- **Small marbles** slip into the sponges, get temporarily trapped, and exit much later.
- **Medium marbles** partially enter the sponges → they exit in between.

The column of gel beads works **exactly like this**. The gel matrix (e.g., **Sephadex G-75**, **Sephacryl S-200**, **Bio-Gel P-100**) acts as a "molecular sieve."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** In Gel Filtration, separation is governed **purely by molecular size**, NOT by chemical affinity, charge, or polarity. This makes it uniquely suited for **native molecular weight determination** under non-denaturing conditions.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Void Volume** | $V_0$ | Volume of mobile phase in the spaces **outside** the gel beads |
| **Elution Volume** | $V_e$ | Volume of mobile phase required to elute a specific molecule from the column |
| **Total Bed Volume** | $V_t$ | Total volume of the packed column |
| **Internal Volume** | $V_i$ | Volume of mobile phase trapped **inside** the gel beads |
| **Available Fraction** | $K_{av}$ | Fraction of internal volume accessible to a given molecule |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Reference Molecule:** **Blue Dextran (MW ≈ 2,000,000 Da)** is used to determine $V_0$ because it is **completely excluded** from all gel pores due to its enormous size.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle of Operation — The "Why" and "How"

The separation mechanism can be broken down into **five sequential logic steps**:

1. **Sample Loading:** A small volume of the macromolecule mixture is carefully layered on top of the gel bed without disturbing the surface.
2. **Partition into Pores:** As the mobile phase (buffer) flows downward (or upward), each molecule distributes itself between the **external mobile phase** and the **internal pore volume** based on its size relative to the pore size of the gel.
3. **Selective Exclusion:** Molecules **larger than the largest pores** are completely excluded → they travel only through the void volume $V_0$ and elute first.
4. **Partial/Total Penetration:** Molecules **smaller than the smallest pores** freely diffuse into ALL accessible pores → they experience the maximum path length and elute last at a volume approaching $V_t$.
5. **Intermediate Molecules:** Molecules of intermediate size access only a portion of the pores → their elution volume lies between $V_0$ and $V_t$.

### 2.2 The Three Volumes Relationship

The total column volume is partitioned as:

$$V_t = V_0 + V_i + V_{matrix}$$

Where $V_{matrix}$ is the **solid volume** of the gel beads themselves (negligible in most calculations but conceptually important).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Alert:** Only $V_0$ and $V_i$ contain mobile phase. A molecule's elution position depends on the **fraction of $V_i$ it can access**, which is the $K_{av}$ value.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $K_{av} = \dfrac{V_e - V_0}{V_t - V_0}$ | Available fraction (available to a molecule) | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | $K_d = \dfrac{V_e - V_0}{V_i}$ | Distribution coefficient (pore penetration) | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 3 | $V_t = \pi r^2 h$ | Total geometric column volume | mL (or cm³) |
| 4 | $\log(MW) = m \cdot K_{av} + c$ | Calibration line equation (linear) | MW in Daltons (Da) |
| 5 | $V_e = V_0 + K_{av}(V_t - V_0)$ | Rearranged elution volume equation | mL |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The $K_{av}$ value is **always** between **0 and 1**. A value of **0** means the molecule is **fully excluded** (elutes at $V_0$). A value of **1** means the molecule is **fully included** (elutes at $V_t$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Quality control of therapeutic proteins (insulin, monoclonal antibodies, erythropoietin).
- **Biotechnology R&D:** Aggregation analysis and oligomer detection in recombinant protein production.
- **Polymer Science:** Molecular weight distribution profiling of biopolymers.
- **Food Industry:** Characterization of polysaccharide hydrolysates and dairy proteins.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the $K_{av}$ Equation

**Step 1: Define the accessible volume for a given molecule.**

A molecule of a specific size can access only a portion of the internal volume $V_i$. The volume of internal gel accessible to the molecule is:

$$V_{accessible} = V_e - V_0$$

> *Logic:* The molecule travels through $V_0$ (excluded pore space) PLUS the part of $V_i$ it can enter. The total liquid volume traversed equals $V_e$.

**Step 2: Define the maximum accessible volume for a fully penetrating molecule.**

A very small molecule can access the **entire** internal volume $V_i$. The maximum available internal volume is:

$$V_{max\_accessible} = V_t - V_0$$

> *Logic:* Subtract the void volume from the total volume to get all the liquid that is "available" to penetrate.

**Step 3: Form the ratio (the $K_{av}$ definition).**

$$K_{av} = \frac{V_{accessible}}{V_{max\_accessible}} = \frac{V_e - V_0}{V_t - V_0}$$

> *Logic:* This ratio is the **fraction of available internal volume** that the molecule actually penetrates. It is dimensionless and bounded between 0 (fully excluded) and 1 (fully included).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Calculation

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Numerical):**
A gel filtration column has the following parameters:
- Total bed volume: $V_t = 50$ mL
- Void volume determined using Blue Dextran: $V_0 = 18$ mL
- An unknown protein elutes at: $V_e = 32$ mL

**Calculate:** (a) The $K_{av}$ of the unknown protein. (b) If the calibration equation from standards is $\log(MW) = -1.8 \, K_{av} + 5.4$, determine the molecular weight of the unknown protein.

---

**Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

**Part (a): Calculate $K_{av}$**

$$K_{av} = \frac{V_e - V_0}{V_t - V_0}$$

Substituting values:

$$K_{av} = \frac{32 - 18}{50 - 18}$$

$$K_{av} = \frac{14}{32}$$

$$K_{av} = 0.4375$$

> *Interpretation:* The unknown protein can access about **43.75%** of the available internal pore volume.

---

**Part (b): Calculate Molecular Weight**

Using the calibration equation:

$$\log(MW) = -1.8 \times K_{av} + 5.4$$

Substituting $K_{av} = 0.4375$:

$$\log(MW) = -1.8 \times 0.4375 + 5.4$$

$$\log(MW) = -0.7875 + 5.4$$

$$\log(MW) = 4.6125$$

Taking antilog:

$$MW = 10^{4.6125}$$

$$MW \approx 40,973 \text{ Da} \approx 41 \text{ kDa}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Standard Proteins Used for Calibration

| Standard Protein | MW (Da) | $\log(MW)$ | Expected $K_{av}$ Range |
|------------------|---------|------------|-------------------------|
| **Blue Dextran** | 2,000,000 | 6.301 | 0.000 (defines $V_0$) |
| **Thyroglobulin** | 669,000 | 5.825 | 0.05 – 0.20 |
| **γ-Globulin** | 158,000 | 5.199 | 0.20 – 0.40 |
| **Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA)** | 67,000 | 4.826 | 0.30 – 0.50 |
| **Ovalbumin** | 44,000 | 4.643 | 0.40 – 0.60 |
| **Carbonic Anhydrase** | 29,000 | 4.462 | 0.50 – 0.70 |
| **Cytochrome c** | 12,400 | 4.093 | 0.70 – 0.90 |
| **Vitamin B₁₂** | 1,355 | 3.132 | ≈ 1.0 (fully included) |

### 3.4 Calibration Curve Construction Logic

A standard calibration experiment follows this **sequential protocol**:

1. **Column Preparation:** Pack the column with degassed gel slurry; ensure no air bubbles. Equilibrate with **2–3 column volumes** of running buffer.
2. **Void Volume Determination:** Apply **Blue Dextran (0.2% w/v)**. Collect fractions. The elution volume at peak $A_{280}$ (or 620 nm) absorbance is $V_0$.
3. **Total Volume Determination:** Measure the geometric column volume $V_t$ directly from the column dimensions OR use a small molecule (e.g., vitamin B₁₂) to mark $V_t$.
4. **Standard Calibration:** Run a series of proteins of known MW individually. Record $V_e$ for each. Calculate $K_{av}$ for each.
5. **Plot the Curve:** Plot $\log(MW)$ on **Y-axis** vs $K_{av}$ on **X-axis**. Fit a straight line via least-squares regression.
6. **Unknown Sample:** Apply the unknown under **identical conditions**. Determine $V_e$, calculate $K_{av}$, then read off $\log(MW)$ from the calibration line, and compute $MW = 10^{y}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Define Gel Filtration Chromatography. Explain why Blue Dextran is used to determine the void volume of a gel filtration column.**

**Model Answer (Point-wise, KTU-Board Style):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Gel Filtration Chromatography (GFC), also called Size Exclusion Chromatography (SEC), is a chromatographic technique that separates molecules based on their **hydrodynamic size** using a column packed with porous gel beads as the stationary phase.
2. **Blue Dextran Property (1 mark):** Blue Dextran is a high-molecular-weight polysaccharide (MW ≈ 2 × 10⁶ Da) that is **completely excluded** from the pores of all commonly used gel matrices (Sephadex, Sepharose, Bio-Gel).
3. **Reason for Use (1 mark):** Because it cannot enter the gel pores, it travels **only through the void volume ($V_0$)** — the space outside the beads. By detecting its elution spectroscopically (at 620 nm or by absorbance peak), $V_0$ is accurately determined.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**What is $K_{av}$? State the formula and explain the significance of its numerical range (0 to 1).**

**Model Answer (Point-wise):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** $K_{av}$ (available fraction or available distribution coefficient) is the fraction of the internal gel pore volume that is accessible to a given solute molecule.
2. **Formula (1 mark):**
$$K_{av} = \frac{V_e - V_0}{V_t - V_0}$$
3. **Significance (1 mark):** A value of **0** indicates the molecule is **fully excluded** (elutes at $V_0$, very large MW). A value of **1** indicates the molecule is **fully included** (elutes at $V_t$, very small MW). Intermediate values indicate partial pore penetration, linearly correlated with $\log(MW)$.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Describe the **principle, apparatus, and procedure** for the determination of the molecular weight of a protein using **gel filtration chromatography**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained for a gel filtration column (Sephadex G-100) calibrated with standard proteins. Using the data, construct a calibration curve and determine the molecular weight of an unknown protein that eluted at $V_e = 28$ mL. Take $V_0 = 15$ mL and $V_t = 50$ mL. **(7 marks)**

| Standard | MW (Da) | $V_e$ (mL) |
|----------|---------|------------|
| Cytochrome c | 12,400 | 42 |
| Ovalbumin | 44,000 | 32 |
| BSA | 67,000 | 25 |
| γ-Globulin | 158,000 | 19 |

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Principle, Apparatus, and Procedure (7 marks):**

- **Principle (2 marks):** GFC separates molecules on the basis of their **size and shape** using porous gel beads as the stationary phase. Large molecules are excluded from pores and elute first; small molecules penetrate pores and elute last. The elution volume $V_e$ of a protein is inversely related to its molecular size and is used to determine molecular weight via a calibration curve.
- **Apparatus (2 marks):**
  * Glass column with sintered glass bottom (e.g., 1.5 × 50 cm).
  * Sephadex G-100 (or suitable gel) pre-swollen in running buffer.
  * Peristaltic pump / gravity feed system.
  * Fraction collector (1–2 mL fractions).
  * UV-Vis spectrophotometer (set at 280 nm for protein).
  * Blue Dextran (2,000,000 Da) for $V_0$ determination.
- **Procedure (3 marks):**
  1. Pack the column evenly with degassed gel slurry; avoid air bubbles.
  2. Equilibrate with 2–3 column volumes of buffer (e.g., 0.05 M phosphate, pH 7.0, with 0.15 M NaCl).
  3. Determine $V_0$ by loading 0.5 mL of Blue Dextran solution and locating the peak elution volume.
  4. Apply individual standard proteins (1–2 mg/mL in 0.5–1 mL volume); collect 1 mL fractions; measure $A_{280}$; record $V_e$ at the peak.
  5. Apply the unknown protein under identical conditions; record its $V_e$.
  6. Calculate $K_{av}$ for each standard and the unknown using the formula.
  7. Plot $\log(MW)$ vs $K_{av}$ for standards; fit a straight line; read off the MW of the unknown.

**(b) Calibration and MW Determination (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate $K_{av}$ for each standard using:
$$K_{av} = \frac{V_e - V_0}{V_t - V_0} = \frac{V_e - 15}{50 - 15} = \frac{V_e - 15}{35}$$

| Standard | MW (Da) | $V_e$ (mL) | $K_{av}$ | $\log(MW)$ |
|----------|---------|------------|----------|------------|
| Cytochrome c | 12,400 | 42 | 0.771 | 4.093 |
| Ovalbumin | 44,000 | 32 | 0.486 | 4.643 |
| BSA | 67,000 | 25 | 0.286 | 4.826 |
| γ-Globulin | 158,000 | 19 | 0.114 | 5.199 |

**Step 2:** Linear Regression (two extreme points used for simplicity; full least-squares gives similar answer):

Using γ-Globulin and Cytochrome c as endpoints:

$$m = \frac{5.199 - 4.093}{0.114 - 0.771} = \frac{1.106}{-0.657} = -1.683$$

$$c = 5.199 - (-1.683)(0.114) = 5.199 + 0.192 = 5.391$$

Calibration line: $\log(MW) = -1.683 \cdot K_{av} + 5.391$

**Step 3:** Calculate $K_{av}$ for unknown ($V_e = 28$ mL):

$$K_{av} = \frac{28 - 15}{50 - 15} = \frac{13}{35} = 0.371$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into calibration line:

$$\log(MW) = -1.683 \times 0.371 + 5.391$$

$$\log(MW) = -0.624 + 5.391 = 4.767$$

$$MW = 10^{4.767} \approx 58,500 \text{ Da} \approx 58.5 \text{ kDa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The unknown MW of ~58.5 kDa falls between BSA (67 kDa) and Ovalbumin (44 kDa), which is consistent with its $V_e$ of 28 mL being between their respective elution volumes. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Explain the **selection of gel matrix** for a molecular weight determination experiment. List four commonly used gel materials and their fractionation ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gel filtration column has $V_0 = 20$ mL and $V_t = 60$ mL. An unknown protein elutes at $V_e = 38$ mL. The calibration equation obtained from standards is $\log(MW) = -2.1 \cdot K_{av} + 5.6$. Calculate the molecular weight of the unknown protein and state whether it is closer to a 25 kDa or 70 kDa protein. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Gel Matrix Selection (7 marks):**

- **Selection Criteria (3 marks):** The gel matrix is selected based on:
  1. **Molecular weight range of target molecules** — the fractionation range of the gel must encompass the MW of the unknown.
  2. **Sample type** — whether proteins, nucleic acids, or polysaccharides.
  3. **Buffer compatibility and chemical stability** — must tolerate the chosen buffer and pH.
  4. **Bead rigidity and flow rate requirements** — affects resolution and run time.

- **Four Common Gel Materials (4 marks):**

| Gel Material | Manufacturer | Fractionation Range (Da) | Best For |
|--------------|--------------|--------------------------|----------|
| **Sephadex G-50** | Cytiva/GE | 1,500 – 30,000 | Small peptides, small proteins |
| **Sephadex G-100** | Cytiva/GE | 4,000 – 150,000 | Medium proteins |
| **Sephadex G-200** | Cytiva/GE | 5,000 – 600,000 | Larger proteins, enzymes |
| **Sephacryl S-200** | Cytiva/GE | 5,000 – 250,000 | High-resolution protein work |
| **Bio-Gel P-100** | Bio-Rad | 5,000 – 100,000 | Alternative to Sephadex G-100 |

**(b) MW Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate $K_{av}$:

$$K_{av} = \frac{V_e - V_0}{V_t - V_0} = \frac{38 - 20}{60 - 20} = \frac{18}{40} = 0.45$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the calibration equation:

$$\log(MW) = -2.1 \times 0.45 + 5.6$$

$$\log(MW) = -0.945 + 5.6 = 4.655$$

**Step 3:** Calculate MW:

$$MW = 10^{4.655} \approx 45,200 \text{ Da} \approx 45.2 \text{ kDa}$$

**Step 4:** Comparison with reference proteins:
- Distance from 25 kDa: |45.2 − 25| = 20.2 kDa
- Distance from 70 kDa: |45.2 − 70| = 24.8 kDa

> **Conclusion:** The unknown protein is **closer to 25 kDa** in molecular weight, but lies in an intermediate range. The actual MW is **~45.2 kDa**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $K_{av}$ with $K_d$:** $K_d = (V_e - V_0)/V_i$ requires knowing $V_i$; $K_{av}$ uses $V_t$ which is **easier to measure geometrically**. KTU expects $K_{av}$ unless $V_i$ is explicitly provided.
> 2. **Inverting the calibration plot:** Always plot $\log(MW)$ on the **Y-axis** vs $K_{av}$ on the **X-axis** so that the slope is **negative** (higher $K_{av}$ → smaller molecule → lower MW).
> 3. **Forgetting the antilog:** Final answer must convert from $\log(MW)$ to MW using $MW = 10^{y}$, where $y$ is the y-value from the calibration line.
> 4. **Unit confusion:** $V_0$, $V_e$, $V_t$ must all be in the **same units** (mL or cm³). Mixing mL with L is a frequent error.
> 5. **Skipping the regression step:** For full marks, show the calculation of slope (m) and intercept (c) explicitly.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Technique:** Gel Filtration Chromatography (GFC / SEC) — separates macromolecules by **size only**, not by charge or affinity.
- **Elution Order:** **Large molecules first**, **small molecules last** (inverse of their size).
- **Void Volume ($V_0$):** Determined using **Blue Dextran 2000** (MW ≈ 2 × 10⁶ Da); it occupies only the space outside the gel beads.
- **$K_{av}$ Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):**
$$K_{av} = \frac{V_e - V_0}{V_t - V_0}$$
- **Range of $K_{av}$:** Always between **0** (fully excluded) and **1** (fully included).
- **Calibration Curve:** Plot **$\log(MW)$ vs $K_{av}$** — gives a **straight line with negative slope**.
- **Gel Selection:** Choose gel so that target MW lies in the **middle of the fractionation range** (for best resolution).
- **Buffers:** Use non-denaturing buffers (e.g., phosphate buffer + 0.15 M NaCl) to maintain native MW.
- **Key Constants to Remember:**
  * Blue Dextran MW = **2,000,000 Da**
  * Cytochrome c MW = **12,400 Da**
  * BSA MW = **67,000 Da**
  * Ovalbumin MW = **44,000 Da**
  * γ-Globulin MW = **158,000 Da**
- **Linear Range:** Calibration is linear only when the unknown MW lies **within the linear portion** of the standard curve (i.e., $K_{av}$ between ~0.1 and ~0.9).
- **Final Answer Conversion:** Always report MW in **Daltons (Da)** or **kilodaltons (kDa)** after antilog calculation.
- **KTU Exam Pattern:** Numerical problems with 4–5 standard proteins, asking for $K_{av}$ computation, regression, and final MW of an unknown — practice with at least **3 numerical drills** before exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
